claims.claim_text: The opening fintech migration and outage story is presented as a real March 2023 incident with precise upgrade versions, API removal, outage duration, and transaction impact.
claims.anchor_text: In March 2023, a Series B fintech startup migrated their payment processing platform from a self-managed Kubernetes cluster on Compute Engine VMs to Google Kubernetes Engine. The migration took three weeks. On day one of production traffic, an engineer noticed that GKE had automatically upgraded the control plane from 1.26 to 1.27 during a peak traffic window. A deprecated API that their Helm charts still referenced---`autoscaling/v2beta2`---was removed in 1.26. The rolling update caused 14 minutes of degraded service for their checkout flow, impacting an estimated $340,000 in transactions. The post-mortem revealed two failures: first, they had enrolled in the Rapid release channel without understanding its upgrade cadence. Second, they had never tested their manifests against the next Kubernetes minor version. The CTO's summary: "We chose a managed service to avoid operational surprises, and then we did not read the manual."
claims.suggested_rewrite: A team can still suffer upgrade-related outages on a managed Kubernetes service if they choose an aggressive release cadence and don't test their manifests against the next Kubernetes version before production rollouts.
claims.rationale: This is an unattributed incident narrative with named timing, versions, duration, dollar impact, and a quote.

claims.claim_text: The module says the GKE control plane is 'zero cost' in Standard mode, with one free zonal cluster per billing account and a management fee only for regional clusters.
claims.anchor_text: - **Zero cost**: Google does not charge for the control plane in Standard mode (one free zonal cluster per billing account; regional clusters incur a management fee).
claims.suggested_rewrite: - **Cluster fee and free tier**: GKE charges a cluster management fee, and the free tier provides monthly credits equivalent to one free Autopilot or zonal Standard cluster per billing account; regional cluster fees aren't covered by that credit.
claims.rationale: Current GKE pricing uses cluster management fees plus free-tier credits, so the 'zero cost' phrasing is outdated.

claims.claim_text: Regional Standard and Autopilot control planes have a 99.95% SLA, while zonal Standard control planes have a 99.5% SLA.
claims.rationale: The GKE pricing page explicitly lists these financially backed availability figures.

claims.claim_text: The table claims zonal control plane upgrades cause about five minutes of downtime while regional upgrades are zero-downtime rolling upgrades.
claims.anchor_text: | **Control plane upgrade** | ~5 min downtime | Zero downtime (rolling) |
claims.suggested_rewrite: | **Control plane upgrade** | Temporary control plane unavailability during upgrades | Highly available rolling upgrades with continued API access |
claims.rationale: Google documents the availability difference, but not this exact zonal downtime number or a blanket 'zero downtime' phrasing.

claims.claim_text: The regional-cluster billing-surprise anecdote is presented as a real team incident with a specific `--num-nodes=10` mistake and 30-node outcome.
claims.anchor_text: **War Story**: A team created a regional cluster with `--num-nodes=10` expecting 10 nodes total. They got 30 nodes (10 per zone across 3 zones) and a billing surprise at the end of the month. The `--num-nodes` flag in regional clusters specifies the count **per zone**, not total. Always multiply by 3 for regional clusters.
claims.suggested_rewrite: **War Story**: Regional clusters can create more nodes than teams expect because node counts are distributed across zones. In the default three-zone layout, a `--num-nodes` value applies per zone rather than as a single cluster-wide total, so plan capacity and cost accordingly.
claims.rationale: The operational lesson is valid, but the anecdote itself is unattributed and presented with fabricated specifics.

claims.claim_text: Regional GKE clusters replicate the control plane across multiple zones and, by default, distribute Standard node pools across three zones, so node counts multiply across zones.
claims.rationale: The regional-clusters documentation directly describes replicated control planes and default three-zone worker-node distribution.

claims.claim_text: Node auto-provisioning can create new node pools to satisfy workload requirements and delete auto-created node pools when they become empty.
claims.rationale: The node auto-provisioning documentation explicitly says GKE creates node pools for pending workloads and deletes empty auto-created pools.

claims.claim_text: GKE Autopilot was introduced in 2021 and lets Google manage the cluster infrastructure such as nodes and node pools.
claims.rationale: Google's launch post provides the February 2021 introduction date and describes the Autopilot management model.

claims.claim_text: The billing table says Standard mode pays for running VMs, while Autopilot pays for Pod resource requests.
claims.anchor_text: | **What you pay for** | VMs (running nodes) | Pod resource requests |
claims.suggested_rewrite: | **What you pay for** | Compute Engine nodes | Usually Pod requests for general-purpose workloads, or node-based billing for workloads that request specific hardware |
claims.rationale: Current Autopilot pricing has both pod-based and node-based billing models depending on the workload's hardware selection.

claims.claim_text: The billing table says idle nodes do not apply in Autopilot.
claims.anchor_text: | **Idle nodes** | You pay | Does not apply |
claims.suggested_rewrite: | **Idle nodes** | You pay for node capacity whether Pods fully use it or not | For general-purpose Pod-based billing, you aren't separately managing idle nodes, but hardware-specific Autopilot workloads can still use node-based billing |
claims.rationale: The absolute 'does not apply' wording is too broad because some Autopilot workloads use node-based pricing.

claims.claim_text: The billing table says over-provisioned Pods in Autopilot are billed on requested resources.
claims.anchor_text: | **Over-provisioned pods** | You pay for unused VM capacity | You pay for requested resources |
claims.suggested_rewrite: | **Over-provisioned pods** | Unused node capacity still costs money | Over-requesting Pod resources increases your bill, and some hardware-specific Autopilot workloads use node-based pricing instead |
claims.rationale: The teaching point is correct, but the table's absolute wording omits Autopilot's node-based billing cases.

claims.claim_text: The billing table says the minimum Autopilot charge is based only on Pod requests.
claims.anchor_text: | **Minimum charge** | VM cost (even with 0 pods) | Pod requests only |
claims.suggested_rewrite: | **Minimum charge** | Node cost applies even when the cluster is mostly empty | Billing depends on the Autopilot model in use; general-purpose workloads are request-based, while hardware-specific workloads use node-based pricing |
claims.rationale: This row is too absolute for current Autopilot pricing behavior.

claims.claim_text: Spot pricing is available in Standard mode via Spot node pools and in Autopilot via Spot Pods.
claims.rationale: The GKE Spot VMs documentation explicitly covers Spot node pools and notes that Spot Pods are an Autopilot feature.

claims.claim_text: In Autopilot, you cannot SSH to underlying nodes because the underlying VMs are not directly accessible to customers.
claims.rationale: The cluster-architecture documentation states that Autopilot underlying VMs are not visible or directly accessible.

claims.claim_text: Autopilot blocks privileged containers and host namespaces by default.
claims.rationale: The Autopilot security documentation explicitly says Autopilot blocks privileged containers and host namespaces by default.

claims.claim_text: The table's DaemonSet row says Autopilot initially disallowed DaemonSets and only allowed them since late 2022 with restrictions.
claims.anchor_text: | No DaemonSets (initially) | Node management is Google's job | Allowed since late 2022 with restrictions |
claims.suggested_rewrite: | DaemonSets that need elevated node access are restricted | Google manages the nodes | Use only DaemonSets that comply with current Autopilot constraints or approved allowlisted workloads |
claims.rationale: The historical timing is unsupported here and the current product behavior is more nuanced than the row suggests.

claims.claim_text: Autopilot doesn't let customers choose custom node images; nodes use a GKE-managed operating system image.
claims.rationale: The cluster-architecture page states that Autopilot nodes use a GKE-managed Container-Optimized OS and customers don't manage node OS selection.

claims.claim_text: The table says resource requests are required in Autopilot.
claims.anchor_text: | Resource requests required | Billing and scheduling | Always specify requests |
claims.suggested_rewrite: | Resource requests strongly influence billing and scheduling | GKE uses or adjusts requests when sizing infrastructure | Explicitly specify requests so Autopilot doesn't rely on defaults or automatic adjustments |
claims.rationale: Autopilot can apply defaults and adjustments when requests are omitted, so 'required' is too strong.

claims.claim_text: The table says the maximum is 110 Pods per node in Autopilot and that this cannot be changed.
claims.anchor_text: | Max 110 pods per node | Kubernetes default | Cannot be changed |
claims.suggested_rewrite: | Pods per node are pre-configured by GKE | Scheduling density depends on the selected node configuration | You can't directly tune this setting in Autopilot |
claims.rationale: Current GKE documentation says Standard defaults to 110 Pods per node, while Autopilot uses pre-configured limits that vary by configuration.

claims.claim_text: The module says Standard can be cheaper than Autopilot once utilization reaches 85%+ with tuned node pools and Spot instances.
claims.anchor_text: The math flips when utilization is high. If your Standard cluster runs at 85%+ utilization with well-tuned node pools and Spot instances, Standard can be cheaper than Autopilot.
claims.suggested_rewrite: The math can flip when utilization is consistently high. If your Standard cluster is tightly tuned and uses capacity efficiently, Standard can sometimes be cheaper than Autopilot.
claims.rationale: The exact 85% threshold is an uncited benchmark rather than a stable documented cutoff.

claims.claim_text: Rapid, Regular, and Stable release channels have the timing and intended-use properties shown in the table, including Rapid being several weeks after upstream GA and Regular being the default recommendation for most users.
claims.rationale: The release-channels documentation directly describes channel timing, default status, and recommended use cases.

claims.claim_text: Clusters enrolled in a release channel are automatically upgraded for both the control plane and nodes.
claims.rationale: The release-channels documentation states that GKE automatically upgrades cluster control planes and nodes in release channels.

claims.claim_text: Auto-repair is enabled by default for new Standard node pools and repairs nodes after conditions such as `NotReady` for about 10 minutes or boot-disk exhaustion for about 30 minutes.
claims.rationale: The node auto-repair guide documents both the default setting and the repair criteria with these approximate thresholds.

claims.claim_text: VPC-native clusters are the default for new GKE clusters.
claims.rationale: The VPC-native clusters documentation explicitly states that VPC-native is the default mode for new GKE clusters.

claims.claim_text: The first 'Did You Know' item claims an internal codename, a February 2021 launch date, more than a year of prior internal use, and 40-60% idle node capacity among most customers.
claims.anchor_text: 1. **GKE Autopilot was internally code-named "serverless GKE"** before launch in February 2021. Google's internal teams ran Autopilot-like clusters for over a year before making the feature public. The design philosophy came from observing that most GKE customers left 40-60% of their node capacity idle because they over-provisioned "just in case."
claims.suggested_rewrite: 1. **GKE Autopilot launched in February 2021** as a mode of operation designed to reduce manual node management and improve how infrastructure is matched to workload needs.
claims.rationale: Only the public launch timing is readily supportable here; the codename, internal-use duration, and customer-idleness statistics are not.

claims.claim_text: The second 'Did You Know' item says the GKE control plane runs on Borg and that control-plane components are scheduled as Borg tasks.
claims.anchor_text: 2. **The GKE control plane runs on Borg**, Google's internal cluster management system that predates Kubernetes. When you create a GKE cluster, the control plane components (API server, etcd, controller manager) are scheduled as Borg tasks in Google's own infrastructure, completely isolated from your project's resources. This is why you cannot see or access the control plane VMs.
claims.suggested_rewrite: 2. **The GKE control plane runs in Google-managed infrastructure** that is separate from your project's worker nodes. That's why customers interact with the control plane through managed endpoints and don't directly access the control plane VMs.
claims.rationale: The supported documentation covers Google-managed control-plane infrastructure, not the specific Borg implementation claim.

claims.claim_text: The third 'Did You Know' item claims GKE processes over 4 billion container deployments per week as of 2024 and is the largest managed Kubernetes service by deployment volume.
claims.anchor_text: 3. **GKE processes over 4 billion container deployments per week** across all customers as of 2024. It is the largest managed Kubernetes service by deployment volume. The sheer scale means Google catches bugs and performance issues in the Kubernetes codebase faster than any other operator, which is why GKE patches often ship before upstream fixes are widely available.
claims.suggested_rewrite: 3. **GKE operates at very large scale inside Google Cloud**, which gives Google significant operational experience running Kubernetes for many customers. Avoid uncited deployment-volume rankings or causal claims about patch timing here.
claims.rationale: These deployment-volume and ranking claims aren't backed by an obvious allowlisted primary source in this form.

claims.claim_text: The fourth 'Did You Know' item says maintenance exclusions can block upgrades for up to 180 days and that clusters can fall off a release channel into 'no channel' status.
claims.anchor_text: 4. **Maintenance exclusions can block upgrades for up to 180 days**, but there is a catch. If you block upgrades for too long, your cluster can fall off the release channel entirely and enter "no channel" status. At that point, you lose the managed upgrade experience and must manually manage versions. Google recommends keeping exclusions under 30 days to avoid version skew problems.
claims.suggested_rewrite: 4. **Maintenance exclusions are bounded by release-channel support rules**. Short "No upgrades" exclusions are limited, and longer exclusions must still end by the minor version's end-of-support date, so you can't postpone upgrades indefinitely.
claims.rationale: Current GKE documentation describes 30-day 'No upgrades' limits and end-of-support bounds, not this 180-day and 'fall off the channel' formulation.

claims.claim_text: The common-mistakes table says Autopilot defaults resource requests to 500m CPU and 512Mi memory when you omit them.
claims.anchor_text: | Running Autopilot without resource requests | Assuming defaults are optimal | Autopilot sets default requests of 500m CPU / 512Mi memory; specify your own for accurate billing and scheduling |
claims.suggested_rewrite: | Running Autopilot without resource requests | Assuming defaults are optimal | Autopilot can apply default requests when you omit them, but the defaults vary by workload class and might not match your needs; specify your own for accurate billing and scheduling |
claims.rationale: Current Autopilot defaults vary by compute class, and the general-purpose default memory value is not 512Mi.

further_reading.title: GKE cluster architecture
further_reading.why_relevant: This is the clearest primary reference for control-plane and node responsibilities in Standard and Autopilot.

further_reading.title: Compare features in Autopilot and Standard clusters
further_reading.why_relevant: This comparison page is the fastest way to verify which cluster mode supports specific operational or security features.

further_reading.title: About release channels
further_reading.why_relevant: This explains the real channel cadence, upgrade behavior, and maintenance-exclusion constraints that the module discusses.

notes: Skipped quiz prompts, exercise steps, code blocks, and Mermaid diagrams per instructions. Repeated claims were consolidated where the same fact reappears outside quiz and lab sections.
